content:
This is the root of the problems facing the modern AI industry — and beyond.

8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Andrea De Santis on UnsplashWe humans are like large language models. In fact, we are the same as them.
That’s the view of Geoffrey Hinton, winner of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics. In reality, however, he has no connection to physics. He is an expert in a different field — machine learning.
Nor is he an expert in linguistics. Nevertheless, he claims to understand language better than linguists do. He calls Noam Chomsky’s theory — one of the cornerstones of modern linguistics — stupid. He offers no evidence to refute this theory. He simply claims that it is stupid.
Geoffrey Hinton’s Heavy Artillery
Finally, Hinton is neither a cognitive scientist nor a neuroscientist. But that doesn’t stop him from claiming that he understands how the brain works. He believes he knows how real biological neurons function and how thought arises.
So you shouldn’t be surprised by the confidence with which he discusses what consciousness is.
In this regard, he even outshines the world’s leading experts who have devoted their entire lives to studying this phenomenon. While they claim that we know almost nothing for certain about this subject, Hinton knows more than just that. He even knows that ChatGPT or Claude are conscious. And he poses a question to the public:
What’s gonna happen when they get smarter than us? ’Cause they are gonna get smarter than us. …And if they get smarter than us, the question is, will they wipe us out? There’s several reasons for thinking they might.
Oh yes. Dario Amodei now has someone to invoke in his theater of AI safety alarmism.
Of course, he could have done this earlier. We have Nick Bostrom, we have Ray Kurzweil, we have Eliezer Yudkowsky.
And a whole bunch of other “experts” who’ve been predicting an AI apocalypse for more than a decade now. As if, all of a sudden, this AI will gain consciousness, look around, and decide that we’re in the way.
And it’ll think: why not turn all these people (and, for that matter, the entire universe) into paper clips?
And as if this scourge weren’t enough, the singularity is lurking just around the corner. It’s that nightmare scenario where AI starts self-improving in a feedback loop, and we’ll quickly lose the ability to understand it.
Dario, of course, is concerned about all this, and Hinton is the perfect choice here as the heaviest-hitting artillery. The “Godfather of AI,” a Nobel laureate, and one of the key contributors to the development of neural networks.
He also mentored Ilya Sutskever — the very man without whom OpenAI wouldn’t exist — and who firmly believed in the scaling hypothesis and promoted it in every way possible.
If you’re not familiar with it, the essence of the hypothesis is that scaling up models makes them «smarter» in much the same way Moore’s Law describes exponential progress. And that, ultimately, this dynamic will lead to the emergence of reason in AI.
In other words, a chatbot will spontaneously transform into AGI.
To Sutskever’s credit, he found the courage to admit his mistake last November. The scaling hypothesis doesn’t work. Scaling models won’t lead to the emergence of AGI. This isn’t the path to the Holy Grail — it’s a dead end.
But what about his mentor?
A Bot That Has Acquired Subjective Experience
And his mentor, as I’ve already noted, insists that AI chatbots — specifically those that use LLMs — possess consciousness.
To avoid appearing to make unsubstantiated claims, he describes an experiment with a multimodal bot equipped with a camera and a robotic arm.
He places an object in front of the test subject and asks it to simply point at it first. When the bot does so, Hinton secretly alters the conditions of the experiment. He places a prism between the camera and the object to mislead the bot. And sure enough, this time the bot points its robotic arm in the wrong direction.
Hinton explains: “No, that’s not where the object is. The objects actually straight in front of you, but I put a prism in front of your lens”
The chatbot replies: “Oh, I see. The prism bent the light rays, so the object’s actually there, but I had the subjective experience it was there.”
Hinton’s conclusion: if the bot uses the words “subjective experience” in precisely this sense, then it uses them exactly the same way we do. This means that multimodal chatbots already possess subjective experience.
To reiterate: if the bot said that it had a conscious experience, then, according to Hinton, the bot truly did have it. And this experience is identical to ours.
The Hard Problem of Consciousness
Hinton then distinguishes between conscious experience and consciousness, without explaining the difference, but asserting that this is the “harder problem.”
He immediately refutes all conceptions of consciousness formed over decades by researchers in this field:
I think the model we have of the mind and of what consciousness is at present is as wrong as the belief that people were designed by God.
In other words, in his view, the results of all this research are prejudices, just like the belief in an afterlife.
But he doesn’t stop there. You remember, right? We are the same as an LLM. He expands on this idea further:
I think in particular because we’re making these new beings [chatbots], it’s going to completely change our view of what people are.
In other words, as I understand it, he’s proposing reverse engineering — but turned inside out. It’s not about understanding how real intelligence works in order to create AI based on that same principle. No — it’s about adopting a definition of intelligence and reason based on what Hinton observes in chatbots.
In fact, this is Hinton’s main approach to AI. He just equates the brain’s functioning with that of an artificial neural network. That is, in his view, the brain’s functioning consists solely of changing the weights of the connections between neurons. That’s it. And none of the insights neuroscience has accumulated over more than a century of theoretical and experimental research.
Oh well, never mind the research. But Hinton simply ignores the anatomy and functions of real biological neurons. And since there’s no need to pay attention to that, there’s also no need to ask questions about how it works in the real world, rather than in the world of his own fantasies.
In other words, in his “study” of the brain, Hinton has reached exactly the level where one can draw an analogy — roughly corresponding to neural networks — between data exchange among brain cells and stop there.
And anything that goes beyond this crude, oversimplified picture is dismissed as a prejudice not worth mentioning.
You might argue that it’s not necessary to understand the workings of the brain in every detail to create AI that rivals real intelligence. I completely agree with this statement. One must always stop at some level of abstraction sufficient to understand the principle of how it works. But Hinton does something entirely different. He substitutes knowledge of the principle with a dogmatic postulate. He simply presents his hypothesis as established fact.
And Hinton seems completely unconcerned that this hypothesis has absolutely no support in the very fields of knowledge that study intelligence, the mind, and consciousness.
Consequences
People will believe anything, as long as that belief promises them a better future.
AI promises us so much that to some it seems like a salvation from all our troubles.
And it’s so tempting to mistake our desires for reality, isn’t it?
Leaders in the AI industry claim that we’ve almost got it all in the bag. We just need even more data centers, more investment, and political support.
Personally, I’m not sure that all these people care about the public good to the same extent that they care about their own profits. But of course, they need to convince the public of exactly that. But how?
Citing recognized authorities is one obvious strategy. When Dario Amodei says that AI is conscious, he implicitly relies on an authority like Hinton. When he claims that AI could destroy us all if we don’t take it seriously, he does the same thing.
When Sam Altman says that we are on the verge of creating AGI, he is echoing Hinton’s definition of intelligence, as interpreted by himself.
The underlying message is that while OpenAI and Anthropic’s models may not yet be intelligent, they will soon be able to become so. So, these companies aren’t inflating an AI bubble; rather, they’re leading humanity to a place where all our problems will be a thing of the past.
So there’s a rationale for pouring all these hundreds of billions of dollars into technology labeled “Artificial Intelligence.”
But all this hype won’t change the essence of the technology. It won’t eliminate its flaws, because they aren’t bugs — they’re features.
The AI bubble won’t turn into a thriving industry bringing enormous wealth to those who were shrewd enough to invest in it.
The outcome will be very different — and you can probably guess how it will end.
Conclusion
But that’s not all.
Imagine a world in which machines have rights comparable to those of humans. This means that these machines will be considered persons, and “harming” them will become a criminal offense.
Would you wish to go to jail for turning off your chatbot?
Would you claim that it will never happen because it’s a completely absurd scenario?
Yes, but if we follow Hinton’s logic, chatbots already possess consciousness, and therefore, they are legal persons. If this idea continues to gain traction, sooner or later, it will be institutionalized. And then even the wildest absurdity could become our reality.
Note that I’m not claiming that a machine cannot possess some form of consciousness. And I don’t deny that, in this case, we humans would indeed have to assume certain moral obligations toward this new form of intelligence.
But these will be machines created on an entirely different basis. Those machines will resemble us — not the other way around, as Hinton believes.
And if this happens, we will have to approach the solution to the truly hard problem of consciousness — a concept put forward by philosopher David Chalmers — with all responsibility of which our human mind is capable.
Undoubtedly, this will be one of the most serious challenges ever faced by humanity in our entire history.
As for Hinton, it seems he simply does not understand the depth of this problem.

Enjoyed the article? Subscribe to my updates to stay in the loop on what’s happening behind the scenes of the AI industry.
